title: The Third Thing Born in the Fire — Jung and the Transcendent Function
author: C. G. Jung

body.text: Jung begins from division — the conscious personality has one attitude, the unconscious another. One side says yes, the other no. Safety against image, control against symbol. The transcendent function is the bridge that forms when opposites are held together long enough for athird thing to be born. Amiddle space where image mediates between intention and depth.
His first professional paper on active imagination links active imagination to work with dreams and the transference relationship.

Active imagination begins in necessity — aconflict too alive for conscious reasoning alone. The unconscious brings images because the ego has reached its map'sedge. Jung treats images as events — afigure, dream, or inner voice approached as acarrier of psychic reality. The ego must enter relation. The image speaks. The ego answers. The tension between them becomes the furnace.

body.caption: The furnace of opposites — the third thing born from sustained tension.

body.text: The confrontation of the two positions generates aquality of conjoined opposites.

Transcendent function is the furnace'sproduct — opposites resolved by the intellect, held in living tension until athird quality emerges that belongs to neither parent. This third thing is imaginal: an image, symbol, or felt shift that carries the conflict forward. Jung calls it transcendent because it reaches beyond the original opposition without denying either pole.
Jung on active imagination describes the method of holding inner figures in dialogue through drawing, writing, and confronting them until the ego'sposition is affected.

The method is simple and difficult. Hold the conflict. Stay in the tension. Draw what arises. Write what the figure says. Let the ego be affected. The transcendent function is technique — it emerges when the psyche is trusted to produce its own medicine.

body.caption: The third thing — neither thesis nor antithesis, but a new figure born from both.

body.text: The third thing is an image that contains more than the ego could generate alone. That is why Jung calls it transcendent — it reaches beyond the mind that held the opposites. The soul produces its own medicine when the furnace is hot enough. The alchemy is already inside the psyche. The fire just needs tending.
